import CodeWrapper from "../../src/components/Mdx/CodeWrapper" import { FontAwesomeIcon } from "@fortawesome/react-fontawesome" import { faSun, faMoon, faBars, faX } from "@fortawesome/free-solid-svg-icons" import { faGithub } from "@fortawesome/free-brands-svg-icons" # Overview #### Utility Classes for SCSS I assume you're familar with the concept of utility classes. If not, it's really easy to pick up. I'm thinking maybe the best way for a quick introduction to the library is with a quick example of the classes in action: ### A quick example: Colors! For a quick intro, let's take a look at the colors. I go more in depth in these docs. Here are the default colors the framework defines by default:

Black

White

Red

Green

Blue

Default

Background

Magenta

Now let's dive into some questions that one might reasonably have: ### "What if I want different colors?" Good Question! I made everything to be customizable, and hopefully easy. Probably one of the first things one would want to do if they had a design, theme, or even just an idea would be to set their colors and fonts of course. Add/remove at will, make your own names, change values, go wild! ### "What about different shades? Dark-mode? Psuedo classes? We got those! It's all in these docs. For more in-depth coverage of this particular subject I'd glance at the [Pseudo Classes](/docs/pseudo-classes/) overview and move on to the [Colors](/docs/colors/) for examples of shades, pseudo-class functionality, etc. For more info on customizing them, see [Customizing Colors and Fonts](/customize/colors-and-font/). But I'd wait just a min before that, because I want to show off a bit more overview! ### "So... what do these classes look like?" Another fine question! Let's take a look at the HTML for those boxes above: ```html

Black

White

Red

Green

Blue

Default

Background

Magenta

``` Each class name cooresponds to a CSS property. Making these docs will require a lot of examples, lots of styles, but since everything is just utility classes, it adds 0 clutter to my stylesheets. None whatsoever. ### Isn't that more typing than 'Normal' CSS? It's a matter of taste, for sure. Less style clutter, possibly more typing. But with the almight ctrl-c, ctrl-v and editor shortcuts, it's not really much of an issue. Plus if you're using a modern frontend framework, you're rarely repeating yourself like this anyway, this is a bit of a special case. The advantages are incredibly slim stylesheets, I can see the styles right in the markup, everything is mostly easy to keep consistat, and it's just satisfying to me! ### "Doesn't already Tailwind do this?" I'm a big admirer of tailwind. But there's differences. See the [Motivation](/motivation/) page! ### "So what exactly do these classes mean?"" Here are the cooresponding css values for all the classes used in the example above: | Class | CSS rule | | :-------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------- | | bg-black | background-color: #000; | | bg-white | background-color: #fff; | | bg-red | background-color: #cb3810; | | bg-green | background-color: #589f50; | | bg-blue | background-color: #2978a0; | | bg-default | background-color: #252832; | | bg-background | background-color: #fdfcf9; | | bg-magenta | background-color: #bf4081; | | text-white | color: white; | | text-black | color: black; | | text-background | color: #fdfcf9 | | font-large | font-family: "Francois One", sans-serif | | m-xl | margin: 1.5rem; | | m-sm | margin: 0.5rem; | | h-16 | height: 16rem; | | w-6 | width: 6rem | | h-6 | height: 6rem | | display-flex | display: flex; | | flex-col | flex-direction: column; | | flex-wrap | flex-wrap: wrap; | | items-center | align-items: center; | | justify-center | justify-content: center; | | center | display: flex;
align-items: center;
justify-content: center; | ### "So, how hard is it to change these Colors (or anything)?" Not very hard. If you want a clase like `bg-purple` for example, it's as simple as adding it to a variable in the SCSS file. Here's what the color variables look like by default: ```scss $colors: ( "default": ( "white": #fff, "black": #000, "red": #cb3810, "green": #589f50, "blue": #2978a0, "default": #252832, "background": #fff8f0, "magenta": #bf4081 ), "dark-mode": ( "background": #292d3a, "default": #dcdbda, "green": #246410 ) ); ``` All the color classes are generated from these. It's as easy as changing this map. Adding a key value like `"purple": #662d91` will in turn will generate `bg-purple` and `text-purple` and `border-purple` etc. You can add/remove, rename if you prefer names like "primary", "secondary", even add modes and change shades. No special ways of doing anything. If you can develop a website, you can change certinaly a variable name! Of course variables such as these generate a large number of classes, which is why I recommend taking a look at the documentation on purging. But hopefully you can see how easy it is to edit this or just about any variable you can imagine. Again, the [Customize](/customize/) section goes into depth a bit more on customizing these and other variables. ### "Anything else I should know?"" I hope you liked this quick example. Glance around the docs, see the [Motivation](/motivation/) page if you want more insight into my twisted mind, see the [Install](/install/) page if you want to use these classes, and feel free to click the magenta icon in the top menu bar to go to the repo!
